content.text: Reliable resource management is one of the most underappreciated aspects of writing production-quality agent code. Every tool that touches a file, database connection, network session, subprocess, or thread lock must guarantee cleanup regardless of whether the body succeeds, raises an exception, or is interrupted — and Python's context manager protocol provides exactly that guarantee through a concise, composable, and idiomatic mechanism.
is any Python object that implements the
special methods. When used inside a
before the indented body runs and calls
after the body finishes — whether it returned normally, raised an exception, or hit a
The simplest mental model: a context manager is a structured bracket. Everything between the opening bracket (
) and the closing bracket (dedent) is the managed scope. The protocol guarantees that the bracket always closes — the cleanup always runs.
Context managers exist in the standard library for files (
), database transactions, temporary directories, patching in tests (
), and much more. Third-party frameworks extend this to HTTP client sessions, async database pools, and distributed tracing spans.

Agent systems are long-running server processes. A single tool invocation that opens a file without closing it, or acquires a lock without releasing it, creates a resource leak. Under load — hundreds of concurrent tool calls — such leaks exhaust file-descriptor limits, starve connection pools, or deadlock workers within minutes.
Without context managers, every acquire/release pair must be wrapped in a manual
block. This is verbose, easy to forget, and often skipped when developers are in a hurry. Context managers push that discipline into the type system: once the object exists, the cleanup is automatic.

In agentic pipelines that fan out across many tools, the cumulative effect of correct resource management matters enormously. A single rogue subprocess left alive consumes CPU. A held database transaction blocks other queries. Context managers make safe resource use the path of least resistance.
runs on every exit path: normal return, exception,
— the indentation visually shows exactly where the resource is live; readers see the lifetime at a glance.
with A() as a, B() as b:
receives the exception type, value, and traceback, enabling selective suppression or enriched error reporting.
— encapsulate any setup/teardown pair in a class or a generator function decorated with
— cleanup happens at a non-obvious point (dedent). Developers unfamiliar with the protocol may not realise
runs and may accidentally rely on a resource after the
generator must yield exactly once; it cannot be re-entered or iterated.
or the multi-manager form on one line.
Not a substitute for explicit cleanup in long-lived objects
— if a resource must survive beyond the calling function's stack frame, a context manager is the wrong tool; use explicit lifecycle methods instead.
silently swallows exceptions. This is occasionally intentional (
) but is a source of hidden bugs when applied carelessly.
The with statement desugars to two method calls
into the following steps (simplified CPython bytecode semantics):
— call enter; bind the return value to the
. If it returns a truthy value, suppress the exception; otherwise re-raise.
If the body returned normally: call
Any class implementing both dunder methods qualifies.
returns the resource (or self) to bind to the
receives three arguments describing any exception in flight and returns a boolean —

import time

class Timer:
    """Measure wall-clock time for a code block."""
    def __enter__(self):
        self._start = time.perf_counter()
        return self           # bound to 'as' variable

    def __exit__(self, exc_type, exc_val, exc_tb):
        self.elapsed = time.perf_counter() - self._start
        return False          # always propagate exceptions

with Timer() as t:
    result = expensive_computation()

print(f"Took {t.elapsed:.3f}s")
Writing full classes for simple setup/teardown is verbose.
wraps a generator function: code before
is the enter phase, the yielded value becomes the
binding, and code after yield (in a

from contextlib import contextmanager
import shutil, tempfile, pathlib

@contextmanager
def temp_workspace() -> pathlib.Path:
    """Provide a temporary directory that is always deleted on exit."""
    path = pathlib.Path(tempfile.mkdtemp())
    try:
        yield path          # caller sees the Path object
    finally:
        shutil.rmtree(path, ignore_errors=True)

with temp_workspace() as ws:
    (ws / "output.json").write_text('{"result": 42}')
    process(ws)
# directory gone here regardless of what process() did
inside the generator is mandatory. If you omit it and an exception occurs in the body, the generator's cleanup code after the yield is never reached. Always wrap the yield in try/finally.
The diagram below shows the full execution path through the context manager protocol, including both the happy path (no exception) and the exception path, and where suppression happens.

attrs.alt: Context manager lifecycle — enter, body, normal exit and exception exit paths

content.text: Key observation: both exit paths (normal and exceptional) pass through
. This single chokepoint is what makes the guarantee unconditional. The only way cleanup is skipped is if the Python process is killed with SIGKILL or a hard segfault.
, environment variable overrides, database rollback fixtures.
Subprocess sandboxes — wrapping a process and ensuring termination on exit.
Do not use context managers when
The resource must outlive the calling function — e.g. a connection stored on an object for later reuse.
You need to re-enter the same resource object multiple times; a context manager is single-shot.
The 'resource' has no meaningful cleanup — a plain function call is simpler.
Use a class when: the context manager has non-trivial state across enter and exit, when you need to inherit or compose managers, or when you want to unit-test enter/exit independently. Use
when the logic is simple setup + yield + teardown with minimal state.

import threading

class ManagedLock:
    """Acquires a threading.Lock and reports contention time."""

    def __init__(self, lock: threading.Lock, name: str = ""):
        self._lock = lock
        self.name = name
        self.held_for: float = 0.0

    def __enter__(self) -> "ManagedLock":
        import time
        t0 = time.perf_counter()
        self._lock.acquire()
        self._acquired_at = time.perf_counter()
        self._wait = self._acquired_at - t0
        return self

    def __exit__(self, exc_type, exc_val, exc_tb):
        import time
        self.held_for = time.perf_counter() - self._acquired_at
        self._lock.release()
        return False   # never suppress

_lock = threading.Lock()

with ManagedLock(_lock, name="cache") as ml:
    cache.update(new_values)
print(f"Waited {ml._wait:.4f}s, held {ml.held_for:.4f}s")

from contextlib import contextmanager
from typing import Generator
import subprocess, signal, os

@contextmanager
def subprocess_sandbox(cmd: list[str], timeout: float = 30.0) -> Generator[subprocess.Popen, None, None]:
    """Run cmd in a child process; kill it on exit no matter what."""
    proc = subprocess.Popen(
        cmd,
        stdout=subprocess.PIPE,
        stderr=subprocess.PIPE,
        preexec_fn=os.setsid,   # new process group for clean kill
    )
    try:
        yield proc
    except Exception:
        proc.kill()
        raise
    finally:
        # Always terminate and reap to avoid zombies
        try:
            os.killpg(os.getpgid(proc.pid), signal.SIGTERM)
        except ProcessLookupError:
            pass
        try:
            proc.wait(timeout=timeout)
        except subprocess.TimeoutExpired:
            proc.kill()
            proc.wait()

with subprocess_sandbox(["python3", "user_script.py"]) as proc:
    stdout, stderr = proc.communicate(timeout=25)
# process is always dead here
This is the most common context manager usage. Without it, an exception before

# Without context manager — fragile
f = open("system_prompt.txt")
try:
    prompt = f.read()
finally:
    f.close()

# With context manager — idiomatic
with open("system_prompt.txt", encoding="utf-8") as f:
    prompt = f.read()

# Multiple files in one with statement (Python 3.10+: parenthesised)
with (
    open("input.txt") as src,
    open("output.txt", "w") as dst,
):
    dst.write(src.read().upper())
Example 2 — dynamic resource list with ExitStack
lets you enter an unknown number of context managers and have them all exit cleanly. This is essential when the set of resources is determined at runtime.

from contextlib import ExitStack
import pathlib

def merge_tool_outputs(paths: list[str]) -> str:
    """Open an arbitrary number of output files and concatenate them."""
    with ExitStack() as stack:
        handles = [
            stack.enter_context(open(p, encoding="utf-8"))
            for p in paths
        ]
        return "\n---\n".join(f.read() for f in handles)
    # All files closed here even if some raises

result = merge_tool_outputs(["out1.txt", "out2.txt", "out3.txt"])
is a tiny context manager that silences specific exception types. Use it for truly expected, benign errors where carrying on is correct.

from contextlib import suppress
import os

# Instead of:
try:
    os.remove("stale_cache.db")
except FileNotFoundError:
    pass

# Write:
with suppress(FileNotFoundError):
    os.remove("stale_cache.db")

A production agentic tool executor needs to: open a tracing span, acquire a semaphore to cap concurrency, write tool outputs to a temporary directory, and ensure everything is cleaned up whether the tool succeeds, times out, or raises. Context managers handle every layer.

from contextlib import contextmanager, ExitStack
import asyncio, tempfile, shutil, pathlib
from opentelemetry import trace

tracer = trace.get_tracer(__name__)

@contextmanager
def tool_temp_dir(tool_name: str) -> pathlib.Path:
    d = pathlib.Path(tempfile.mkdtemp(prefix=f"tool_{tool_name}_"))
    try:
        yield d
    finally:
        shutil.rmtree(d, ignore_errors=True)


async def execute_tool(tool_name: str, inputs: dict, sem: asyncio.Semaphore) -> dict:
    with ExitStack() as stack:
        # 1. Tracing span — records latency and errors automatically
        span = stack.enter_context(
            tracer.start_as_current_span(f"tool:{tool_name}")
        )
        # 2. Concurrency cap — releases the slot on exit
        stack.enter_context(sem_context(sem))
        # 3. Scratch space — deleted on exit
        workspace = stack.enter_context(tool_temp_dir(tool_name))

        span.set_attribute("tool.workspace", str(workspace))
        try:
            result = await _run_tool_impl(tool_name, inputs, workspace)
        except Exception as exc:
            span.record_exception(exc)
            raise
        return result


@contextmanager
def sem_context(sem: asyncio.Semaphore):
    """Wraps asyncio.Semaphore.acquire/release as a sync context manager stub."""
    # In real async code use: async with sem — shown here for ExitStack demo
    sem._value -= 1
    try:
        yield
    finally:
        sem._value += 1
exit order (reverse of entry) is important: the tracing span closes last, so it captures the cleanup time of the workspace and semaphore release.
Mistake 2 — using a resource after the with block

# WRONG
with open("data.txt") as f:
    lines = f.readlines()
more = f.readline()   # ValueError: I/O operation on closed file

# CORRECT — capture what you need inside the block
with open("data.txt") as f:
    lines = f.readlines()
# work with 'lines' outside; file is closed, that's fine
unless you deliberately intend to suppress the exception.
when the number of resources is determined at runtime — it avoids deeply nested
Prefer the parenthesised multi-manager form (Python 3.10+) over nested
blocks for two or three simultaneous resources.
Instrument agent tool context managers with tracing spans so observability tools capture both latency and failures in one place.
free of heavy logic — they should acquire/release, not process data.
Write unit tests that exercise the exception path through your context manager, not just the happy path.
The overhead of the context manager protocol itself is negligible — two additional method calls per
bytecode instructions are highly optimised; benchmarks show sub-microsecond overhead vs. a bare try/finally.
The scalability concern is not the protocol but what happens inside it. Common patterns to watch in high-throughput agent servers:
on every tool call opens and tears down a connection pool each time. Prefer a long-lived client shared across calls, guarded by a single outer
inside a hot tool path becomes a serialisation bottleneck. Profile with
before concluding a lock is needed.
is a syscall. For extremely high call rates (>1 000/s), consider a pooled directory scheme rather than create/destroy per call.
Async context managers in async agents
to avoid blocking the event loop during I/O-bound setup (e.g. acquiring an async database connection).
For async agent servers (FastAPI, Starlette, LangServe) the lifespan pattern is the idiomatic way to manage application-level resources:

from contextlib import asynccontextmanager
from fastapi import FastAPI
import httpx

@asynccontextmanager
async def lifespan(app: FastAPI):
    # Startup — runs before the server starts accepting requests
    app.state.http = httpx.AsyncClient()
    try:
        yield
    finally:
        # Shutdown — runs after the last request is handled
        await app.state.http.aclose()

app = FastAPI(lifespan=lifespan)
standard-library module provides utilities that extend or compose context managers:
— turn a generator function into a context manager.
— dynamic collection of context managers; all exit in reverse order.
— temporarily redirect standard streams (useful in test fixtures).
— a no-op context manager that yields a value; useful as a placeholder when a context manager is optional.
— pytest converts yield fixtures into context managers automatically, making test setup/teardown composable.
wraps a block in a tracing span.
— temporarily bind log fields to all log statements inside a block.
— cancel a block after a timeout in async frameworks (anyio, trio, asyncio).

import anyio

async def tool_with_timeout(query: str) -> str:
    with anyio.move_on_after(10.0) as cancel_scope:
        result = await slow_tool(query)
    if cancel_scope.cancelled_caught:
        return "[timeout]"
    return result
Several patterns achieve resource management in Python. The table compares them on the dimensions that matter most for agent tooling.
No — wraps the call, not a scope
async I/O: DB pools, HTTP sessions

Every open file descriptor, database connection, and network socket represents a finite OS resource. Agent servers that handle many concurrent tool calls hit OS limits quickly when resources leak. Context managers are the primary defence, but they should be paired with OS-level limits: set
appropriately and use connection pool size limits (e.g.
Subprocesses spawned by code-execution tools must always be reaped. An orphaned subprocess in a new process group is invisible to the parent's cleanup code. Always call
(Linux) in the subprocess preexec and use
to kill the entire group on exit, as shown in the subprocess sandbox example above.

A lock acquired in a context manager is released even if the body raises, preventing the classic leak-on-exception deadlock. However, context managers do not prevent logical deadlocks (two threads each holding one lock and waiting for the other). Enforce a consistent lock-acquisition order across all code paths and use

is a potential silent failure point. In agent servers, silently dropped exceptions turn into mysterious missing results with no log trace. Use suppression only for truly benign, expected errors (e.g. deleting a file that may or may not exist) and always log at least at DEBUG level when suppressing.
Network and subprocess context managers must carry timeouts. An agent waiting indefinitely on a hung child process ties up a worker permanently. Use
to bound every I/O-bound context manager, and ensure the context manager's
Think of a context manager as a hotel room key-card system. Inserting the card (
) switches on the power; removing the card (
) cuts all power — air conditioning, lights, everything — regardless of whether you left intentionally or ran out in a fire alarm. You cannot forget to turn things off; the hotel guarantees it.
is a keycard that controls several rooms at once — remove it and all rooms power down in sequence, last-entered first.
is a do-not-disturb sign that tells the hotel to ignore a specific kind of interruption.
runs on every exit path — normal return, exception,
— making it the only safe way to manage resources in agent code.
Implement context managers as a class (
) for stateful or composable managers, or with
; omitting it means cleanup is skipped on exceptions.
(propagate) unless exception suppression is intentional and documented — accidental suppression is a common source of silent bugs.
handle dynamic numbers of resources; they eliminate deeply nested
blocks and handle partial failures correctly.
to avoid blocking the event loop during setup and teardown.
Always pair subprocess and network context managers with explicit timeouts — without them a hung child process ties up a worker indefinitely.
cover the most common one-off patterns without requiring you to write a class.
